note.soap:
S:
Patient is a 13-17 year old from Georgia presenting with a tick bite and associated fever and chills for 24-72 hours. No rash reported. Pregnancy not possible.

O:
No physical exam available due to phone-only encounter. No rash or other symptoms reported.

A:
  - Working diagnosis (most likely): Early tick-borne illness (e.g., early Lyme disease or other tick-borne infection)
  - Differential:
    1. Early Lyme disease without rash
    2. Other tick-borne infections (e.g., ehrlichiosis, anaplasmosis)
    3. Viral illness unrelated to tick bite
  - Red flags considered:
    - High fever, chills, systemic symptoms
    - No rash or neurological symptoms reported
    - Duration 24-72 hours

P:
  - Disposition: Urgent in-person evaluation recommended
  - Plan:
    - Advise patient to seek urgent in-person medical evaluation for physical exam, laboratory testing (CBC, blood smear, Lyme serology, other tick-borne disease panels), and possible empiric treatment.
    - Educate on signs of worsening illness including rash, joint pain, neurological symptoms, or persistent high fever.
  - Safety net:
    - Instruct patient/guardian to go to emergency department immediately if symptoms worsen rapidly, develop difficulty breathing, severe headache, neck stiffness, or altered mental status.
    - Follow up with primary care or infectious disease specialist within 24-48 hours if symptoms persist or worsen.

Evidence (snippets used):
No direct evidence snippets retrieved; recommendations based on standard clinical practice for tick bites with fever and CDC guidance on tick-borne illness evaluation.
patient_instructions: If you are not improving as expected, or if symptoms worsen, seek in-person medical care.
Medication options you may consider if safe (no dosing by phone): acetaminophen (Tylenol) if you can take it safely; ibuprofen (Advil) if you can take it safely.

evidence.title: CDC: What to Do After a Tick Bite
CDC: Guidance for Clinicians—Caring for Patients after a Tick Bite (PDF)
IDSA/AAN/ACR: 2020 Guidelines for Prevention, Diagnosis, and Treatment of Lyme Disease
AAFP: Lyme Disease—Updated Recommendations from IDSA/AAN/ACR (2021)
